transcript: It was a perfect day to walk down N Avenue and wonder, if building numbers get higher and higher, do they ever stop? And that's exactly what George was doing. Until he got curious about a sound. Ha-ha-ha-ha-ha! Huh? Huh? Ha-ha-ha! Hi, George. Want to shoot baskets? 23! When's it going to be my turn, Steve? Would miss if i miss now where was i oh yeah 23 i'm going for a new record 24 baskets in a row Oh, now it's your turn, Betsy. Here, you take a turn, George. Maybe you can beat Steve's record. That's one. You need 23 more to beat me. George was sure he could get 24, 34, maybe 104. Oh no! Wait! Come back! That's our ball! Huh. Now what? How about miniature golf? Hmm? You've never played? It's great! There's a castle and a windmill. I'll show you how to play. I'm good at this. To figure your score, add up how many times you hit the ball before it goes in the hole. Thank you. To figure your score, add up how many times you hit the ball before it goes in the hole. George had one goal, to do better at golf than he had at basketball. Hmm. By the end of the first hole, George knew he was great at this game. A little softer. On hole number one, I got a five, Betsy got eight, and George twenty-seven. George hadn't come close to Steve's twenty-three baskets, but he already had the highest golf score. on this whole Betsy got four and I got three. If you get it in now, you'll have nine. Nine? George was sure he could get that score up higher. But it took effort to miss from so close. That's ten. Keep trying. Eleven. Twelve. Thirteen. Fourteen. Hit the windmill again. That's 20. Getting a high score at golf was easy. George was born to golf. At the end of the game, Steve totaled up the scores. I had 35. That's my new record for the best score yet. Bessie's adds up to 58. And George, 257.
